sentences.text: Yes.
Skis.
The last year of high school came that we could leave Russia and go to Ukrainian-- to Ukraine.
And we left.
So my last year of high school was-- I graduated from high school in-- not-- in a small town not far from Kiev.
I didn't know how to spell this.
I was delighted.
I was-- I was there only with that hope knowing that one day, we're going to go home.
That was the only thing that made us go-- made my mother, and dad, and my brother.
My brother never went to school, because they couldn't send him to school.
Then he couldn't be with them.
OK.
So this was a part that he really missed in his life, like the rest of his life, not going to school.
I mean, he went to-- when he came to Germany, he went there.
But you could never make up.
I must've been four years, four years.
And in one year, in the Ukrainian school.
Yes.
Yes.
No.
No, no, no.
No.
Not as children.
We participated in all the activities, studying, let them live, let them be well.
Didn't know anything what's going on.
Not me.
And my parents never would tell me anything, so.
Which I didn't previously told you, also my father used to in that home-- what they call [NON-ENGLISH]-- used to be the manager of a farm that belonged to do the [NON-ENGLISH].
So he learned the language.
They both spoke a very good Russian.
But he was so good man for them.
And they got the food, what they had to.
They got the bread they could eat.
They had a beautiful place where they could shower.
They gave them warm clothes, what they needed, and bedding.
And under the circumstances of war, they were fortunate.
No, no, no.
Cleaned.
My father would go out begging, and he would go to small villages, maybe three, four miles.
And one time, he came across a very lovely woman, a farmer.
And he created in his own-- a lie that he has twin children babies, and he needed to milk.
And she really liked my father.

So each time, maybe once a week, or once in two weeks-- I really don't remember-- she would give him some milk in a jug-- milk, maybe a piece of butter, maybe a small little loaf of bread that she made.
And he will come home with that.
So this is what we had to eat.
It didn't last very long.
I don't remember.
I cannot tell you-- a week, or two, or three, or a day.
And the woman had the desire to meet the twins.
Now where does he get twins?
And that was the end of the relationship with that lady.
She didn't know in which tent we were living because I assume if she would know, she probably would have come over herself.
So the people were very nice if they were not under stress of the police, the Russian themself.
They could help share with you as much as they could.
Yes.
At the time, I believe he was so depressed that he had no other choice.
That begging was a natural thing of doing, or else he wouldn't have anything to put in your mouth.
It came very natural.
You learned how to do it.
He was a good provider, like any father, would care for the children.
I mean, if you wake up in the morning, and somebody's crying, is hungry, what do you do if you don't have?
He didn't steal.
It didn't rob anybody.
He legitimate went to good people, knocked on the door.
If they gave him something, he was grateful.
If they didn't, they said, we don't have it.
We can't share.
He said, thank you.
And that was the story.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We were living-- again, they gave us-- the Ukrainian people who had more rooms than one or two, the government made them give up one room.
So we were at very rich people that were also farmers.
They had their own gardens with cherries, with pears, which we were not allowed to shake a tree or get a pear.
They gave us a room-- one room, again, with a little oven.
And there was no glass.
I don't know what they had before in that room.
We had boarded up.
And we slept in that one room-- eight in one room.
As I say that time, my father used to work for the government.
That was already after the dome-- after the home of the invalids.
He used to work on a big farm.
So our main nourishment was a piece of bread and the red beets that they make sugar.
He used to bring them home by a sack on his shoulder.
He managed there.
He was employed by the government as a worker.
Those wages paid a piece of bread, a potato.
He didn't get any money.
But that's what we [? bridged ?] through that year.
And that time, we heard from our Uncle Mendel.
And I graduated.
And we went back to Poland--
--willingly.
Mendel, as I mentioned before, under the circumstances, how he found out from somebody that maybe came before us, there came a telegram.
Mendel alive.
Sarah in Russia.
That's all we had.
And he gave us the address that he's in Sosnowiec, in his hometown.
I don't remember if we did write to him, or if we sent a telegram back.
I have no memory of that.
Yes.
Not really.
I was not involved at all.
I was not involved.
And the Ukrainian was a little harder.
During the class, they would call me capitalist.
They would ask me to describe them how Europe used to be, that we carried-- the worker carried the capitalist on this back.
You know, that we took out from the worker everything what we could.
And that's not the way in Russia.
They tried to convince me to become a [NON-ENGLISH], which they knew that I speak Russian and everything else, and they did not know our intention of going back home.
But I never wanted to commit myself.
I knew what's going to be the problem.
So I said no.
That's was the plain answer.
They didn't like it, but they wouldn't put me to jail as a youngster.
That was a privilege.
For them, was an honor.
We used to wear that uniform with the white and red tie.
And I was the only one who didn't.
But it didn't bother me at the time at all.
I knew I'm different, but I was accepted as a student.
But socially?
Socially, I didn't do much, except if it was something in the school.
But after school, I didn't do anything with them.
After the year, shortly, we graduated.
We packed--
Spring.
I believe so.
I don't remember the journey.
To be honest, I don't remember the whole journey home.
I know we went on trains.
What later, I don't remember.
We didn't come to our hometown because we knew everybody was killed.
There was nobody there.
We couldn't face the Poles anymore, so we went to Krakow.
In Krakow, my Uncle Mendel waited for us.
No, Poland.
Poland.
And in Krakow, the Polish government had again a home, [NON-ENGLISH], for people who came back from Russia.
And they gave us one room.
And we knew it's temporary because our aim was, at the time, to go to Israel.
And as I spoke before, during that period of time, we picked up our aunt, my brother actually, and she was with us.
From there, we went to Austria.
Uncle Mendel was already in Germany.
So the only way to get to Germany, you had to go to Austria.
There was a DP camp in Austria where they kept us there till the Haganah.
That's the Israeli people who helped the people to get over to Germany, and from Germany to Israel.
They came.
They put us-- I have no memory if we were there a long time or a short time.
I have a picture with my mother outside.
The DP camp was yet with wires around.
But we were not deprived of anything.
It was a period just of waiting to transition.
No.
Linpz.
I think it was Linpz.
L-I-N-P-Z.
I'm not sure.
I should've consult my mother.
And they put us on trucks.
And the trucks were covered so there would be no noise.
We were told to be quiet, not to breathe, not to sneeze, not to cough, just to take us over the border.
Because if they came to the border, they showed the documents, the truck driver, that you know, I think by the end of inside, they put some merchandise.
So they didn't look.
I really don't remember deeper in.
And they brought us to Germany.
Well, in Germany, started a different life again.
My parents were in Landsberg in Germany.
I went my way.
I wanted to go to medical school.
So I went to Munich.
I did bring my diplomas from Russia.
There was a Jewish organization who were all-- even my husband was involved.
They-- the guys were older.
We took an entrance exam to them.
They quizzed me everything, and I passed that.
[POLISH] in Polish, [POLISH]-- the students, basically old, elderly one.
Not elderly.
You know, five, seven years older, that they were already at the university.
And I passed my exam with them.
But I needed Latin also, see which they didn't teach in Russia.
Those days, if you did write the prescription, you wrote yourself in Latin.
So I went to a cloister and rang the doorbell.
And a father came out.
And I told him my story.
And I said, I need some tutoring for the entrance exam.
And he spent with me now almost every day for six months.
And I got a little, as much as I needed it.
And at the time, I really wanted to become a pharmacist.
But in Munich, everything was destroyed.
So there was an opening on dentistry.
And that's what I accepted, and I became a dentist.
Yes.
As a matter of fact, most of them.
And this year, after 50 years, we have a reunion with all of them who survived now living in Israel, October the 24th.
It was the dentists, doctors, engineers, architects because we all were a close family.

And we used to get, while in school, from United States through our organization, every month, a pack of cigarettes, spam, peanut butter-- which till now I won't eat it because I had so much those days.
The cigarettes took us every place.
The cigarettes, we could pay monthly rent with a pack of cigarettes at a time.
We could go to an opera-- for a ticket, a pack of cigarettes.
And this bridged us through.
We didn't have to pay tuition because we were survivors-- for the university.
And we used to get a free lunch through the Jewish organization.
So we were like one big family.
For lunch, we all knew we have to get together and get our one lunch.
So we're very close.
I think we're planning like 124 people to be at the reunion with their spouses.
So it's exciting.
I met my husband-- if I can tell you just a little story?
Which you probably would enjoy.
He was on medicine.
He was medicine.
He was a medical student.
I was on dentistry.
But the space was limited.
And he probably will tell you his story.
He was injured during the war.
He was shot.
So he had the privilege to a seat always in the auditorium.
He had his own seat.
But his own seat was almost on the top of the auditorium, the seats.
For me to get-- we had skin lectures together, dentistry and medicine.
For me to get there in time and grab a seat was like to get up 4 o'clock in the morning.
I had to take street-- that was Munich-- street cars, so forth, so on.
So I decided it's not worth it.
I mean, you know, you have to be alert all day.
So I used to stay in the back.
There were no seats available.
And one day-- I have a picture of here.
I show it to you-- I and my girlfriend stood behind him.
And I said in Polish.
[POLISH], means not friend-- colleague, would you kindly move a little bit?
I had nerves to ask.
And he moved.
And since then, we're sharing the chair.
That's the way our romance started.
And we now married 46 years.
Yes.
Yeah.
That doesn't mean I didn't have other offers from other students.
But somehow, he was-- I looked up to him.
He offered me a lot of things.
I think at the time, I learned from him a lot.
He was a little older, which it doesn't mean-- but he was intelligent.
And I looked to him-- I looked to him up, and I think it was a mutual way of falling in love.
I hope he will admit the same thing.
But I really do.
And I love him till this day.
Previous.
Yes.
He just had the power to take me away from everybody, which it must've been very easy.
And I-- he got the target.
We got married in-- we got married March 20, '49.
I must have known him for two years before.
We got married in my parents' town, Landsberg-- Landsberg, [NON-ENGLISH].
That's Germany.
My mother prepared, and her friend, a doctor's wife-- her name was Blau-- prepared the whole wedding.
And all the students, our friends that we had, we chartered buses from Munich.
And they all came to Landsberg for the festivity.
Landsberg?
Maybe an hour and a half by train.
The bus maybe a little longer.
I don't remember.
Yes.
Mm-hmm.
No, my father at the DP camp had the little store in the camp They had like a basement there where he used to sell groceries-- flour, sugar, fruit.
That's the way he made a living.
Oh, yes.
I had a wedding gown.
I had a beautiful wedding.
I had the real religious wedding.
They made me even go to the mikveh, if you know what this is.
Which I refused, but I had to go, or else the rabbi wouldn't marry us.
Well, it's that basin.
It's supposed to be clean, and used to purify yourself.
I guess in the old times, they didn't have the facilities, so that was maybe the only time that they have to be clean.
But you have to purify.
They dunk you, said a prayer.
I don't remember what kind.
I was scared for the water like hell, because they dunk your head under.
And I was afraid for the water.
They cut your fingernails.
They had to be beautiful and clean.
That's all I remember about it.
And that came a document to the rabbi that I am pure, and it was OK for him.
My dress was a very pretty gown.
My parents-- my mother, bought it for me.
I have a picture, if you would like to take the picture.
We dressed up-- I dressed up my husband at the time very beautiful.

We didn't have anything, but if you want to know the episode, what do we did, you could buy on the market, on the black market, the pure wool American blankets that the army had it-- each soldier, or whatever.
I don't remember how much I paid, but I bought a green blanket.
They dyed it for me, and made it navy.
And the tailor made him a navy coat, which I have it on the picture.
We bought a lovely navy hat.
The tailor made him pretty shoes.
He had a navy suit.
And there he was, all dressed up for the wedding.
Yes, so did his friends.
Yes.
It was easy.
The Germans-- the German, it was a normal life.
They lived a normal life.
There were stores.
You only had to have the money.
You want to know a better story?
Well, now the days when you got married, you needed a nightgown, and I didn't have a fancy one.
So my mother, they were selling parachutes, the white ones.
She bought one, went to the German seamstress.
And I still have it.
And she made me from the parachute a white nightgown.
And that was my nightgown for the wedding night.
The wedding night, all the guys, my parents, they all were in the same hotel.
They all were knocking on the same wall.
And it was fun.
We-- it was fun.
But the next day, we had to go back to school, so we never had a honeymoon.
We never had one, actually, because we couldn't afford.
The schooling was important.
The graduation came in 19-- 1950, May.
We graduated.
My parents were already in United States.
And six months later, going through the American consul and all the things, we were good people to enter the beautiful country we live now in.
No, we went the same through the HIAS, just the way my parents.
The country was so great, so beautiful, and so free.
My mother brought to me the first letter.
They came to United States just before Passover.
They send it to Milwaukee, thinking of all the breweries that my father will be able to get with no problem any job.
And when they came in, they prepare a two bedroom apartment with welcome cakes, with beds, with blankets, with clothing, with groceries in the refrigerator.
I cried over that letter months.
I couldn't believe that's true.
But that's what the country can do.
If you can appreciate your freedom, and you have your goals, the sky was the limit.
You could accomplish whatever you want to in this great, beautiful country we are.
No.
No.
It was yet in Germany.
And actually, my father bought a few machines that they will produce like the type of a soft drink, like Coca-Cola, but made from fruit.
Because besides the beer, he had this at home also to do the same thing in Israel, but not beer.
Bernie, my husband, as a survivor, the only one from the family, had no desire to go to Israel.
He had no desire to go to Israel because, I tell you what, at the time, the people are very pushy at those days in Israel.
They're aggressive.
And he is not this kind of a type of a person.
He's not aggressive.
He cannot stretch his elbows.
And he thought he's never going to make it there.
I made my $50 on the ship called-- it was a military ship-- You had to tell what you did before.
I said I'm a dentist.
My husband was already a physician.
Everybody had to do a job, help it out.
So they opened for me one on the deck a room.
They had all equipment for dentistry.
That was for the sailors.
Well, it was never opened.
So I told them I'm a dentist.
They opened all the machines.
I have a picture of it.
I took one of the-- I befriended myself on the ship with a lady who used to be a nurse before the war.
She was my assistant.
I don't remember.
It's a shame.
But I don't.
She was much older than I am.
And we worked on the sailors.
That was my first step.
They were all drilled.
I couldn't say anything.
The only thing which they taught me is open mouth.
I couldn't pronounce tooth right because you had to speak with your tongue for the T-H.
And they rewarded me with a dollar, $2.
So my first money that I came to New York was $50.
That was great.
From the tooth fairy.
The officers were very generous to us.
They introduced us to a club sandwich, which we didn't know what it was.
They had Coke machines that other people didn't get it, but I guess because we worked, and I basically all day was with them, so they would give us some Coke to drink.
As I say, the club sandwich.
But I learned very quick that that's wrong.
And that started our journey.
I have to mention also the, the feeling at dawn arriving to New York and seeing the Statue of Liberty.
We're all in nighties and underwear because they announced we're coming in.
It was an impressive sight.
There are no words.
There's nothing that you just can express.
It's a new country.
Something Statue of Liberty that we read on the boat, what it meant and what it is.
And that was our new life to start.
Very much so.
Very much so.
From the first day of stepping on that gorgeous soil.
August 5?
Sometimes in August of-- sometimes in August of '45.
Yes.
'49-- '39, we got married.
'49, We got married. 1950.
August 1950.
The United States.
